title: Ratatouille
subtitle: The Provençal stew of aubergine, courgette, pepper, and tomato
excerpt: Ratatouille is a Provençal vegetable stew: aubergine, courgette, pepper, and tomato cooked long in olive oil until they collapse into something silky and deeply savoury. The key is cooking the vegetables separately before combining — it takes more time, but the flavours stay distinct rather than becoming a uniform mash.
sourceNotes:
• Escoffier, *Le Guide Culinaire* (1903, public domain). Early codification of the Provençal vegetable dishes that ratatouille descends from, including the role of olive oil and tomato as the base of southern French cookery.
• Elizabeth David, *French Country Cooking* (1951). David's account of Provençal cooking, and her insistence on cooking each vegetable separately before combining, informs the method here — the step most often skipped and most worth preserving.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freezes well for up to 3 months. Defrost overnight and reheat gently. The texture softens slightly on freezing — acceptable, but the freshly made version has more definition.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles easily. Use the largest wide pan available and cook the vegetables in the same separate batches.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Better the next day. Make up to 3 days ahead and refrigerate. Eat cold, at room temperature, or reheated gently.

glossaryTerms.definition: A Provençal vegetable stew from Nice, made from aubergine, courgette, pepper, tomato, onion, and garlic cooked slowly in olive oil. The name derives from the French 'touiller', to stir. The dish is served hot, warm, or cold, and improves with time.

content.text: has a reputation for being simple, and it is — but only if you cook each vegetable separately before combining. If everything goes in the pan together, the courgettes give off liquid, the aubergines steam rather than fry, and the result is a uniform, watery mush. Thirty extra minutes of cooking in batches is the difference between a good ratatouille and a mediocre one.

Use good olive oil in quantity. Ratatouille is an oil-rich dish and the flavour depends on it. The tomatoes provide the acidity and moisture that brings the whole thing together — use ripe ones, or tinned plum tomatoes if it is winter.

items.prepNote: roughly chopped; or 1 tin of plum tomatoes

content.text: Serve as a main with bread, as a side to grilled meat or fish, or cold the next day as part of a spread. A handful of torn basil added at the end is optional but good.

Set a large frying pan over a high heat with a generous glug of the {{olive-oil}}. In batches, fry {{aubergine}} of aubergine cubes for 6 to 8 minutes until golden and soft. Season and transfer to a large saucepan. Fry {{courgette}} of courgette in the same pan for 4 to 5 minutes. Transfer. Fry {{pepper-red}} of pepper pieces for 5 minutes until beginning to colour. Transfer.

In the same frying pan, soften {{onion}} diced onion in a little more olive oil over a medium heat for 6 minutes. Add {{garlic}} of garlic and cook for 2 minutes. Add {{tomato}} of tomatoes and cook over a medium-high heat for 8 minutes until the tomatoes break down into a rough sauce.

Add the tomato base to the large saucepan with the fried vegetables. Add {{thyme-fresh}} of thyme and {{bay-leaves}} bay leaves. Stir gently to combine without breaking up the vegetables. Cook over a low heat, covered, for 20 minutes. Remove the herbs, taste, and season. Rest for 10 minutes before serving.

items.cause: The vegetables were not fried separately, or the pan was too crowded and they steamed.
items.fix: Fry in small batches over high heat. Each batch should sizzle immediately on contact with the oil. If the pan is crowded, the vegetables release moisture faster than it can evaporate.

items.cause: Undercooked — aubergine needs longer than it looks like it should.
items.fix: Fry the aubergine cubes until a deep golden colour and soft throughout, not just coloured on the outside. They will absorb a lot of oil before releasing it as they cook. Don't add more oil — wait it out.

items.symptom: Everything has cooked into a uniform mash
items.cause: Combined too early and overcooked, or all vegetables were added to the pan at once.
items.fix: Keep the final simmer gentle — 20 minutes over a low heat is enough. Stir carefully. The separate frying step is what gives each vegetable some structural integrity before the final combine.

content.text: Ratatouille is from Nice, though every village in Provence has a version. The vegetables in it (aubergine, courgette, pepper, tomato) are the summer produce of the south of France: sun-heavy, cheap in season, and abundant. Elizabeth David wrote about Provençal cooking before anyone else in England thought to, and her insistence on the separate frying step as the point of difference between a good ratatouille and a mush has been repeated in every reliable recipe since. The dish is as good cold as it is hot, which is rare for a stew and is part of why it has lasted.
